season but this year one of those friends is not at the table we lost a very dear friend yesterday their dear friend ernie rader passed away last week at the age of 76 his life full of friends fishing and farming ernie married for 29 years losing his wife to cancer just months ago leaving six children behind he used to come here every thursday every friday every sunday [Laughter] he loved it here they all did they all do back in march that was the last time they all gathered here around the table he did have and when he was undergoing his dialysis his heart just started slowing down his friends say ernie had lots of health problems and covid they say was just too much leaving them to wonder what will happen next it's so it's just scary scary time we have to get smarter i think everybody just needs to live their life you know if you're living in fear you're not really living but when it comes to dealing with the pandemic one of the biggest questions that so many people are grappling with close down the economy or open back up and let things flow again people who are afraid to wear masks who are not social distancing who are not following the guidelines is promoting this i think we really need to weigh what it's costing the nation versus what it's doing for the public but there needs to be leadership from the top one good plan listen to the science yes listen to the science they're smarter than all the rest of us it's going to take that i don't think the economy can survive with a pullback we have to get the pandemic under control to get the economy under control and as the debate rages on about balancing people's lives on the nation's economy there's one thing these winter texans can all agree on ernie was a friend through and through treated everybody very very well he's just a very generous person and we loved him dearly we're going to miss him a group of friends torn apart by the coronavirus now left to wonder what the government will do next to ensure not only their safety but the safety of others while balancing the economy in aransas pass i'm paul mueller chris 6 news and how fortunate are those friends to still be gathering together thank you to ernie's friends for sharing his story coming up next week paul takes us to san patricio county to a family owned operation known for its barbecue and brisket so what's the word around the tables there find out next tuesday on chris 6 sunrise

Very important was keeping water lines from freezing, and the solution was simple. Run water through your sinks, tubs and showers 24\7. Running water doesn't freeze. You might get a shock from your next water bill, but much cheaper than hiring a plumber. If you had a fireplace use it. It's also amazing what you can find to burn in an emergency. Last, during the power rotations, and when it was your turn, run every electrical appliance you have that produces heat, just remember to turn them off when total power is restored. One other item. - 10 Best Places to Visit in Texas - Travel Video: Kind: captions Language: en They say that everything is bigger&nbsp; in Texas, and it is certainly true&nbsp;&nbsp; that there is a large range of things do&nbsp; so, see and explore in the second largest&nbsp;&nbsp; state. The landscape changes dramatically&nbsp; throughout the expanses of the Lone Star State,&nbsp;&nbsp; from deserts and scrublands to swamps, pine&nbsp; forests, and mountains. Texans are fiercely proud,&nbsp;&nbsp; and they have a lot to be proud of. Whether you’re&nbsp; interested in history, art or cowboy culture,&nbsp;&nbsp; it’s all found in Texas. Here’s a look&nbsp; at the best places to visit in Texas: Number 10. Palo Duro Canyon. Cutting through the High Plains region of Texas&nbsp; is the Palo Duro Canyon, the second largest in the&nbsp;&nbsp; United States, after the Grand Canyon. Red rock&nbsp; is exposed, creating a beautiful sight, but the&nbsp;&nbsp; canyon is more than just a void. It is also where&nbsp; Native American tribes like the Apache and the&nbsp;&nbsp; Comanche once called home. There is an abundance&nbsp; of wildlife in the park, so as you hike the miles&nbsp;&nbsp; of trails, keep your eyes peeled for things&nbsp; like whitetail deer and even mountain lions. Number 9. Fort Worth. Located just a stone’s throw away from Dallas,&nbsp; wandering around Fort Worth is like stepping&nbsp;&nbsp; into the Wild West and it is sometimes known&nbsp; as ‘Cowtown’ due to its cowboy roots. There are&nbsp;&nbsp; various cowboy and cowgirl museums scattered&nbsp; around town where you can learn more about&nbsp;&nbsp; the local history and culture. You can even go to&nbsp; watch a rodeo or cattle drive if you feel like it.&nbsp;&nbsp; If cowboy culture isn’t your thing then head to&nbsp; the fantastic Kimbell Art Museum which has some&nbsp;&nbsp; lovely paintings by Picasso and Goya, or take&nbsp; a walk around one of the city’s numerous parks. Number 8. El Paso. Lying on the border with Mexico, El Paso is the&nbsp; Westernmost city in Texas and as it is located&nbsp;&nbsp; in the desert, it has over 320 days of sunshine a&nbsp; year. An increasingly popular place both to live&nbsp;&nbsp; and visit, El Paso is home to a very welcoming and&nbsp; friendly population. Surrounded by desolate yet&nbsp;&nbsp; beautiful desert landscapes, there are loads of&nbsp; great outdoor activities to be had that vary from&nbsp;&nbsp; rock climbing to cycling and horseback riding.&nbsp; Trips to Ciudad Juarez just across the border&nbsp;&nbsp; were once popular but have become too&nbsp; dangerous due to drug-related violence. Number 7. Big Bend. The aptly named Big Bend National Park&nbsp; in Texas is so-called because it’s set&nbsp;&nbsp; in a dramatic meander of the Rio Grande.&nbsp; On one side of the river is Texas, USA,&nbsp;&nbsp; and on the other side is Mexico.&nbsp; Almost the same size as Rhode Island,&nbsp;&nbsp; Big Bend is known for it’s diversity and has loads&nbsp; to discover on its many trails. The Chisos Basin&nbsp;&nbsp; is the most popular area of the park and features&nbsp; mountain views, but the Chihuahuan Desert – home&nbsp;&nbsp; to real-life roadrunners and coyotes – is&nbsp; really the place to escape into nature. Number 6. Dallas. The state’s third largest city, Dallas is&nbsp; probably best known for the infamous assassination&nbsp;&nbsp; of former U.S. President John F. Kennedy. Located&nbsp; in the same building from which Lee Harvey Oswald&nbsp;&nbsp; fired the shots, the Sixth Floor Museum is&nbsp; dedicated to the assassination. The city&nbsp;&nbsp; is a shopper’s dream thanks to more stores per&nbsp; capita than any other American city. You can also&nbsp;&nbsp; visit one of the many amusement parks in Dallas or&nbsp; attend a local rodeo for some local Texas flavor. Number 5. Galveston. Lying just off of Texas’ Gulf Coast, Galveston&nbsp; is a beautiful place that delightfully combines&nbsp;&nbsp; Southern charm with lovely beaches and loads&nbsp; of stunning nineteenth century architecture.&nbsp;&nbsp; Located on Galveston Island, the resort&nbsp; city is very popular with Houstonians&nbsp;&nbsp; who flock there during the weekend and summers to&nbsp; enjoy the warm weather and laidback way of life,&nbsp;&nbsp; while most cruise ships heading to Texas stop&nbsp; by in its large port. Galveston is the perfect&nbsp;&nbsp; place to make a splash, so don’t miss the free&nbsp; Bolivar Ferry rides or a walk along Pleasure Pier. Number 4. Hill Country. Located In the very center of the state, the Hill&nbsp; County shirks Texas' reputation for being dry and&nbsp;&nbsp; flat, with rolling hills, oak trees, spring-fed&nbsp; creeks and flowing rivers. The whole region is&nbsp;&nbsp; a popular retreat and resort area, with some&nbsp; wonderful hill views and lake swimming, and&nbsp;&nbsp; some good places to camp. Head to Fredericksburg&nbsp; for a dose of German culture, since the area was&nbsp;&nbsp; settled by German settlers in the 19th century.&nbsp; The best time to visit the region is in spring&nbsp;&nbsp; when the Hill Country is ablaze with wildflowers&nbsp; including the Texas State Flower; the Bluebonnet. Number 3. Austin. Known for its slogan, “Keep Austin Weird,” this&nbsp; city definitely feels a little different from&nbsp;&nbsp; the rest of Texas. The population is youthful,&nbsp; thanks in part to the enormous campus of the&nbsp;&nbsp; University of Texas, and it also serves as the&nbsp; Live Music Capital of the country. Austin is&nbsp;&nbsp; the capital of the Lone Star State, so don’t&nbsp; miss the chance to tour the capitol building,&nbsp;&nbsp; which is famously pink and even&nbsp; taller than the U.S. Capitol Building.&nbsp;&nbsp; Also worth exploring are the&nbsp; paths that wind along Barton Creek&nbsp;&nbsp; and the Congress Avenue Bridge, where you can&nbsp; see more than one million bats fly out at sunset. Number 2. Houston. As the biggest city in Texas, Houston is one of&nbsp; the most popular places to visit in the state.&nbsp;&nbsp; Famously known for being home to&nbsp; the NASA Mission Control Center,&nbsp;&nbsp; Houston is also where you’ll find places&nbsp; like the beautiful campus of Rice University&nbsp;&nbsp; and the high-end shopping of the Galleria&nbsp; Area. Many of the city’s biggest attractions&nbsp;&nbsp; are located in the Warehouse District, so&nbsp; head there to see the incredible Downtown&nbsp;&nbsp; Tunnel System or soak in the views from&nbsp; the 60th floor Chase Tower Observatory. Number 1. San Antonio. The jewel of Texas, San Antonio is a city&nbsp; steeped in both rich history and modern day&nbsp;&nbsp; appeal. Located in south-central Texas, the city&nbsp; is one of the largest in the United States and&nbsp;&nbsp; best known as the home to the Alamo. Another&nbsp; of San Antonio’s most visited attractions is&nbsp;&nbsp; the Riverwalk, a vibrant pedestrian scene of&nbsp; restaurants, shops and entertainment venues&nbsp;&nbsp; stretching along both sides of the San Antonio&nbsp; River right in the middle of the downtown area.

.5) Half of the US Navy's submarines, 1/4 of all jet engines and 1/5 of helicopters IN THE WORLD are made in Connecticut. - BTW, the first submarine was invented in New London, CT.
1.) History: Although vehemently denied by the Smithsonian, the TRUE FIRST IN FLIGHT honor goes to Gustave Whitehead who flew over 1/2 mile in Bridgeport, Connecticut in 1901, 2 years before the Wright Brothers. There are plenty of investigative reports that bear this out, some are on YouTube, & the State of Connecticut & the City of Bridgeport legislated a special day honoring Gustave Whitehead for this achievement.
2.) Climate: Conn. has the stable range of all 4 seasons (without the danger you see on the news of some areas in the US) with beautiful foliage in the Autumn, try Route 7 up the Housatonic River, dotted with Covered bridges, restaurants and antique shops all the way to the Mass. border.
3.) Sports: 2 AHL Hockey Teams in Bridgeport & Hartford (newly renovated stadium), 2 Baseball Teams in Hartford and Norwich, CT. (Hartford Stadium described as best new stadium in MiLB). There are 4 ski Mountains in Conn., 4 NASCAR Racetracks including Lime Rock Speedway which is frequented by more than a few movie stars. There are over 170 Golf Courses in CT both public & private.
4.) Universities: Home of perennial NCAA National Champions UCONN & Quinnipiac, also Yale University in New Haven, CT now rated among the top 10 Universities IN THE WORLD. Connecticut also runs its own State-level High School System that will teach your children a trade of their choice such as Nursing, Plumbing, Manufacturing, CAD design & more already PAID FOR INCLUDED BY YOUR TAXES and help them qualify for a Professional License. How's that for a good start for any 18-year-old? This state-run High School System has its own campuses and sports teams that play against the local High Schools and other State High Schools around Connecticut.
5.) Vineyards: There are over 35 Vineyards in Conn., with award winning vintages. Some even have their own restaurants making you feel like you're in France!
6.) Military: CT is the home of the US Coast Guard in New London, CT and also a US Navy Submarine base in Groton, CT. BTW, you can tour a real Navy sub FOR FREE at this Naval base in Groton.
7.) More history: Capt. Kidd is noted as depositing his treasure on an island up the Connecticut River and along the Housatonic River near Stratford & Shelton, CT. Ancient history has a historical site called "Gungywamp" near Groton, CT and is said to date all the way to about 500 AD & is credited to ancient Celtic explorers.
8.) Travel: The are over 30 airports & airfields in Conn., some offering Regional and International flights.
9.) Zoos & Wildlife exhibits: There is Beardsley Zoo in Bridgeport plus 2 Maritime Museums in Norwalk & Mystic, Connecticut, with live hands on exhibits. There are also smaller ones like at Hammonasset State Park along the Connecticut Coastline. And of course, the Zoos in NYC, Rhode Island & Boston are an easy drive from Connecticut.
10.) Proximity: As mentioned, NYC & Boston are an easy drive from anywhere in CT (1-1/2 hrs. on average), so you can work, visit, and enjoy what they offer, but don't have to live in the middle of their overcrowding, crime and resource starved cities.
11.) The Connecticut coast is dotted with almost 50 islands, (off Norwalk & Branford CT) and 14 lighthouses, some you can AirBnB, others have homes, lighthouses, one was the Summer White House of President Taft and another island has a US Wildlife Lab & pavilion you can access by water taxi, and the Park Rangers there will tour you the island for free. There are water-tours available of these islands called the Thimble Islands.
12.) Connecticut is home of famous stars such as Meg Ryan, Michael Bolton, Paul Newman, the Carpenters, Katherine Hepburn, JP Morgan (yes, that one), Suzanne Collins (from the Hunger Games), Daniel Webster (Webster’s dictionary), PT Barnum (Barnum & Bailey 3 Ring Circus), Charles Goodyear (Goodyear Tire & Rubber Co.), Secretary of State Henry Kissinger and the Mayor of Mexico City both lived in Litchfield County, Conn., Cartoonist Gary Trudeau, NBC’s Jane Pauley, Actress Glen Close, Family Guy creator Seth MacFarlane, US President George Bush & still others.
13.) Agriculture #1: The 3 largest dairies in Conn. have over 2,000 cows each and are a very large supplier to famous cheeses like Cabot cheese & others. Some of these dairies you can tour for free - they use huge rotary milking platforms to do 'their work' and each herd happily co-operates!
14.) Agriculture #2: Connecticut is the nation's largest supplier of black currant and a major (very major supplier) of: lavender, apples & sunflowers.
15.) Castles: There are 7 castles in the State, besides the huge mansions that people build, some are in State Parks, along the Connecticut River, some you can tour and rent out for your affair, and one is due for an extensive renovation in Danbury.
16.) The Connecticut coast has 14 lighthouses, 1 is on it’s own island, some you can tour & at least 1 you can stay overnight in (Old Saybrook)
17.) If casinos are your thing, Foxwoods, Mohegan Sun & MGM are about as famous as you’re going to get, all in the Mystic CT area.
18.) There are a plethora of Museums & Theaters in Conn.: New Haven (British Art Museum for one), Hartford Art Athenaeum (50,000 pieces of art from Caravaggio, Cole & Van Dyck), Bridgeport has 4 museums, and just saw Jay Leno in the Warner Theater in Torrington, CT and also the Palace Theater in Waterbury, CT.

- UNSEEN TEXAS - Hidden Places You Won't Believe Exist!: Kind: captions Language: en Since 1845, when Texas joined the Union as the 28th state, it has fiercely guarded its most extraordinary treasures. While millions know its cities and highways, few have witnessed the prehistoric waterways of Kado Lake, where ancient cypress trees rise from waters that predate human settlement. Even fewer have stood before Lighthouse Rock, a geological sentinel that has watched civilizations rise and fall. And at Fort Worth Stockyards, the tradition of cattle drives continues as it has since the 1800s, preserving the spirit of the frontier. Stay with me because we're about to uncover Texas's hidden wonders. Places so extraordinary that you'll wonder why no one ever told you about them before. Palo Duro Canyon is the second largest canyon in the United States and is often referred to as the Grand Canyon of Texas due to its size and impressive geological formations. It is renowned for its multicolored rock layers, steep mea walls and hoodus which are unique rock formations created by erosion. The name Paloduro meaning hardwood in Spanish was given by early explorers because of the abundance of msquite and juniper trees in the canyon. Historically, the park served as a seasonal home for Native Americans who also made durable boughs from the trees. In 1874, Colonel Ranold S. McKenzie led a military expedition that forced Native Americans out of the canyon during the Red River War, marking a turning point in regional control. Its infrastructure was later built by a depression era workforce utilizing local materials. [Music] The lighthouse rock is a sandstone hudoo that stands out for its resemblance to a lighthouse. Its layered structure shows different types of rock stacked on top of each other with each layer representing a different time period with interesting shifts in climate and ancient ecosystems. Pawsom Kingdom Lake is recognized for its clear water and steep limestone cliffs formed by the Morris Shepard Dam. The dam originally operated as a hydroelectric plant until its decommissioning in 2013 and the lake is now known for recreational activities instead. A major attraction is Hell's Gate, a scenic limestone formation that serves as a popular gathering spot for boers and hosts events like cliff diving competitions. El Paso is a border city famous for its desert landscape, cultural connections to Mexico, and historical significance. Established around a Spanish mission, it later became a crossroads for stage coach routes and railroad expansion. The city played a role in the Mexican Revolution with revolutionaries using it as a base. During the 19th century, the city earned the nickname six shooter capital due to its lawlessness during its boomtown era, especially with the event of legendary outlaw John Wesley Harden being taken down there in a saloon. Later in the prohibition era, smuggling operations thrived in the area as alcohol crossed the border. Today, it is more commonly known as the sun city due to its abundant sunshine and is a hub for Texmex cuisine and cowboy boot making. Franklin Mountains State Park is recognized as the largest urban wilderness park in the United States. The park and its representative mountain range are named after Benjamin Franklin Coons, an early land owner in the area who operated a ranch and served as the first postmaster of what later became El Paso. It is also home to the star on the mountain, a massive illuminated star on the mountain. The northeast slope of North Franklin Mountain was home to the only tin mine ever established in the United States, which operated briefly, but was ultimately unsuccessful. Interestingly, the park contains some of the oldest exposed rocks in the region, dating back over a billion years. In the late 1970s, local advocates successfully lobbyed to protect the mountains from development, leading to the creation of the state park in 1987. Monahan's Sand Hills State Park is recognized for its expansive dunes of fine quartz sand, offering a unique desert-like landscape in West Texas. In fact, the sand hills are part of an ancient seabed known as the Perian basin, which was formed at the end of the Paleozoic era. Historically, indigenous groups used the area's water retaining plants for survival, leaving behind grinding stones and fire pits. documented in archaeological surveys. Early settlers attempted ranching but struggled with shifting sands, evidenced by abandoned homestead remnants. The park opened to the public in 1957, preserving a small portion of a much larger dunefield that extends into New Mexico. It became well known for its recreational activities, including sand surfing, sand sledding, and hiking across the everchanging dune formations. Tingua is best known as a ghost town that once thrived as a mining hub and its association with mercury extraction. Established during a mining boom, operations ceased after resource depletion, leaving behind empty processing facilities and worker housing. Decades after abandonment, a small population returned, repurposing old buildings into residences and businesses. An annual chili competition began here, drawing participants despite the remote location. Kad Lake is notable for being one of the largest naturally formed lakes in the region and for its sprawling cypress tree swamp draped in Spanish moss. It is a popular destination for fishing, boating, and exploring its maze of bayou and sloughs. The lake also holds historical importance as a former hub for steamboat travel and early oil exploration. Natural Bridge Caverns is the largest cave system in Texas, offering guided tours that showcase its extensive underground formations, including stelactites, stelagmmites, flowstones, and soda straws. It attracts visitors and scholars who are interested in exploring geological wonders and learning about the natural processes shaping the caverns. San Antonio is a major city that has played a pivotal role in the Texas Revolution and is home to several historic missions including the Alamodome. The city was named after St. Anthony of Padua by Spanish explorers who arrived in the area on June 13th, 1691, the Saints Feast Day. The name was initially given to the river they encountered and later extended to the settlement established nearby. Established around a colonial mission system, it became a focal point for military and cultural exchanges. A pivotal 19th century battle at one of its missions solidified its symbolic importance. The Alamo, originally known as Mission San Antonio Dealero, is San Antonio's most famous landmark, representing Texan independence. Founded in 1718 as a mission to convert Native Americans to Christianity, it later became a fortress. The site gained historical prominence during the Texas Revolution when Texan defenders, including figures like Davy Crockett and James Bowie, were taken down in a 13-day siege by Mexican forces in 1836. The Tower Life building is an iconic structure located in downtown San Antonio, which was once the tallest building in the city. Completed in 1929, it features neo gothic revival architecture with an octagonal design, grotesques that are often mistaken for gargoyles, and a green Ludawi tile roof. Structural engineers identified a slight tilt during construction, but it was corrected using counterweights. A sealed time capsule inside contains cultural artifacts from the exposition era, while a rotating restaurant is placed in the observation tower. Mission Conpsion was designated in 1731 to convert Native Americans to Christianity. As the oldest unrestored stone church in the nation, it is recognized for its original frescos that still adorn its walls and for being one of the best preserved Spanish colonial missions. The mission became part of the San Antonio Mission's National Historical Park in 1978. The Pearl Brewery Complex was originally founded as the San Antonio Brewing Company in 1883. It became one of Texas's largest beer producers until the prohibition movement that banned the sale of alcohol forced it to diversify. After closing in 2001, the site was transformed into a mixeduse cultural hub. It features restaurants, shops, a farmers market, and the Culinary Institute of America campus. San Fernando Cathedral is one of the oldest active cathedrals in Texas and serves as a spiritual and cultural center for San Antonio. Built between 1738 and 1750 by settlers from the Canary Islands, it was significant during the Battle of the Alamo when Mexican General Santa Anna used it as an observation post. The remains of Alamo defenders are believed to be interred here. The cathedral also hosts San Antonio, the saga, a light show projecting the city's history onto its facade. The Edwards Plateau, often referred to as the Texas Hill Country, is known for its rugged terrain, limestone formations, and extraordinary ecosystems. It serves as a vital water source with the Edwards aquifer feeding numerous springs and rivers. The region is also home to the Enchanted Rock, a massive pink granite dome that is one of the largest exposed granite formations in North America. Axis deer are an exotic species introduced from India in the 1930s for hunting purposes. Over time, they escaped captivity and established freeranging populations across the Edwards Plateau. They are easily recognized by their orange coats with white spots that remain throughout their lives, while males grow large antlers with three tines per side. Unlike native white-tailed deer, axis deer breed year round, which allows them to reproduce more frequently. As herbivores, they are also grazers, though they possess a broader diet that enables them to out compete white-tailed deer in areas with limited resources. In fact, their grazing habits can alter vegetation patterns, potentially affecting raparian zones critical for other wildlife. South Padre Island is a barrier island in the Gulf of Mexico. Recognized for its sandy beaches, calm waters, and marine ecosystem. The island was originally called Isla de Santiago, but later became known as Padre Island in honor of Padre Jose Nicolas Bali, a Spanish priest who established the first permanent settlement there while working with the local Carawa people. It is also a major stopover for migratory birds as it sits along the central flyway. a migration route used by thousands of bird species. Therefore, the island has become a hub for outdoor activities such as fishing, bird watching, and water sports. The island also attracts treasure hunters due to its history of shipwrecks and is home to unique natural features like Laguna Madre Bay. Port Isabel located just across the causeway from South Padre Island is a small coastal town with a long maritime history. It served as an important port during the 19th century and remains a gateway to South Padre Island. By the midentth century, Port Isabel earned the nickname shrimping capital of the world due to its thriving shrimping industry. Fort Worth, nicknamed Cow Town, is defined by its deep ties to the cattle industry and as the place where the West begins. It was founded in 1849 by Major Ripley Arnold on a bluff overlooking the Trinity River. The arrival of the Texas and Pacific Railway in 1876 transformed Fort Worth into a major livestock trading center with the stockyards becoming one of the largest livestock markets in the Southwest. Additionally, the fort was central to the famous cattle drives of the late 19th century, being the last major stop for cattle drovers before they took their herds up to Kansas to be shipped by rail. Today, it is celebrated for its western culture, historic sites, and modern attractions. Completed in 1895, the Tarrant County Courthouse is a prime example of Bozar's architecture, known for its detailed stonework and imposing presence. It was conceived by architects Frederick C. Gun and Lewis S. Curtis using pink granite from central Texas. The courthouse's design also includes intricate carvings and a grand dome that stands out against the skyline of the city. However, at a cost of over $400,000, its expense caused public outrage leading to the election of a new county commissioner's court. Over the years, the courthouse has been part of major events in the city's history. From the early days of its growth to more modern legal proceedings, the Fort Worth Stockyards are a historic district that preserves the city's cowboy legacy. Originally established as a cattle market and shipping point, the stockyards area was once home to some of the largest livestock auctions in the country. During its peak from the late 1800s to the mid 1900s, millions of cattle passed through Fort Worth on their way to market. It is characterized by its twice daily cattle drives, rodeos, and western themed attractions. Lake Lewisville is one of the largest reservoirs in North Texas, popular for its recreational opportunities such as fishing, boating, swimming, and camping. It is named after the nearby city of Lewisville, which itself was named after a local businessman, Lewis T. Herring, who helped develop the area in the 19th century. The lake is also recognized as the urban bass fishing capital of Texas due to its abundance of largemouth bass and other fish species. Created by the construction of a dam on the Elmfork of the Trinity River, it was initially built as a water supply reservoir. Although it still serves as a vital water source and provides flood control for nearby cities, it is more well known for its tourism. As the largest city in Texas, Houston is widely recognized for its connection to space exploration, thriving energy industry, and the fight for independence in American history. In fact, it was named after Sam Houston, a military leader who was key to securing Texas's independence. Established in the 1830s, it quickly grew due to its prime location near the Houston Ship Channel, a key trade route. It is home to NASA's Johnson Space Center, where the US space program has made numerous milestones, including the iconic Apollo moon missions. The city is also considered the energy capital of the world due to its significant role in oil and gas. Buffalo Bayou is a slowmoving waterway that runs through Houston, serving as both a historical lifeline and a modern recreational area. It was essential for early trade and transportation with steamboats carrying goods along its waters. Before the era of settlers, tribes like the Karancawa relied on the bayou for sustenance and travel. In the 20th century, the bayou was transformed into the Houston ship channel, cementing Houston's role as an industrial powerhouse. Today it is enjoyed for activities like kaying, biking and hiking along its scenic trails. The Williams Tower is an iconic skyscraper in Houston known for its post-modernist design. At 64 stories tall, it was once the tallest building outside a central business district. Since its completion in 1983, the tower has received multiple awards for its design. However, the public sky deck was permanently closed after September 11th due to security concerns. The Sam Houston monument is a 67 ft equestrian statue located in Herman Park created by Enrico Terraio in 1925. The bronze statue depicts Sam Houston at top his horse pointing forward. It honors his contributions as a military leader and statesman who led forces to victory in the battle of Sanjasinto. Brisket is a centerpiece of Texas barbecue culture, particularly known for its slow smoking preparation over wood like mosquite or hickory. This cut of beef, typically smoked for hours over wood, is considered the crown jewel of barbecue in the region. The cooking process involves slow smoking the meat over wood, often oak or msquite, for hours, allowing the flavors to develop and the meat to become tender and juicy. Historically, German and Czech immigrants introduced meat smoking techniques to Texas in the 19th century with the Jewish communities also contributing by popularizing smoked brisket through delies. By the mid- 20th century, brisket became synonymous with Texas barbecue, with traditional brisket plates usually including simple accompaniment like pickles, onions, and bread to highlight the meat's flavor. Fort Davis is one of the best preserved frontier military posts from the 1800s. It was pivotal in protecting immigrants, male coaches, and freight wagons traveling through the Transpacicos region along the San Antonio El Paso road during the Indian Wars. Strategically located near Olympia Creek in a box canyon surrounded by the Davis Mountains, the site provided ample access to wood, water, and grass for soldiers and their horses. Hamilton Pool Preserve is a natural swimming pool formed thousands of years ago when the dome of an underground river collapsed due to erosion. It is defined by its jade green waters, a 50-foot waterfall that flows year round and its unique grotto surrounded by limestone slabs and stallactites. Interestingly, the waterfall never completely dries up even during droughts, although its flow may reduce to a trickle in dry seasons. Over the years, it has been used for various purposes from recreational swimming to a location for local gatherings. Despite being a popular summer destination, parts of the pool maintain temperatures as low as 50° F due to its natural springfed system. Bandaraa is famously known as the cowboy capital of the world. A title earned from its role as a staging area for cattle drives along the Great Western Trail during the late 19th century. The town celebrates its cowboy culture through rodeos, dude ranches, and historical reenactments, making it a hub for those seeking to experience the Old West. Due to its cowboy culture, it has a reputation for producing some of the best rodeo athletes and ranch hands in the country. Many of these cowboys and cowg girls, often raised in the town, go on to compete in national rodeo events, continuing the legacy of western skills that have defined the area. Today, it hosts an annual 3-day cowboy Mardigra event featuring parades, live music, and western themed festivities that attract thousands of visitors. The coastal city of Corpus Christi attracts people to its beaches and maritime activities as well as is also part of the oil and gas industry. The city was named after the Latin phrase Corpus Christi, which translates to body of Christ by Spanish explorers who arrived in the region on the feast day of the Corpus Christi holiday. During the Civil War, it was bombarded by Union forces as part of efforts to blockade Confederate ports. The city sustained significant damage but remained under Confederate control. Its port is one of the largest in the region, handling millions of tons of cargo annually, vital in prochemical production and international trade. The USS Lexington, nicknamed the Blue Ghost, is a World War II era aircraft carrier. now serving as a museum in Corpus Christi Bay. It participated in several key battles during World War II, including the Battle of the Philippine Sea and raids on Japanese bases, earning 11 battle stars for its service. Decommissioned in 1991, the ship was turned into a floating museum featuring interactive exhibits, restored aircraft, and educational programs. Nues's Bay is an extension of Corpus Christi Bay and serves as an important estie for fish, shellfish, and bird species. It supports various ecosystems including marshes and tidal streams fed by the Nuis River. Unfortunately, heavy industry along the southern shore has contributed to pollution challenges limiting activities like oyster farming. Dallas has a great history regarding the cattle and oil industries which helped build its commercial and urban development. Founded in the 1840s, the area grew rapidly with the arrival of railroads in the 1870s, transforming it into a significant commercial center. It eventually became a hub for the cotton trade in the late 19th century and later expanded into the oil, finance, and technology industries. The city is also famous for its bridges such as the Margaret Hunt Bridge and Margaret McDermott Bridge which span the Trinity River and are celebrated for their architectural design. Interestingly, it also has a significant presence in pop culture with the creation of Barney the Dinosaur and as the setting for the popular TV series Dallas. However, its most iconic event is the 1963 assassination of President John F. Kennedy, which happened when he was riding in a motorcade with his wife in Di Plaza. Reunion Tower, often called the Ball, is an observation tower that dominates the Dallas skyline. Completed in 1978 as part of an urban redevelopment project, it stands 561 ft tall and features a geodic sphere at its top. The geode deck includes interactive displays about Dallas landmarks and telescopes for viewing the city. The tower has appeared in films like Robocop with its LED lighting system, allowing for customizable displays during events. Fredericksburg is a charming Texas Hill country town renowned for its German roots, wineries, and historic sites. The town was named after Prince Frederick of Prussia by John O Musebach who founded the settlement in 1846 under the Society for the Protection of German immigrants. Its annual events such as October Fest and the Gillespie County Fair celebrate its cultural and agricultural traditions while drawing visitors from all over the country. Aranza's Pass is a coastal town that serves as a gateway to Mustang Island and the Gulf of Mexico. Popular for its water-based activities in the shallow bays and nearby Gulf waters. The town is also home to Texas's largest shrimping fleet and hosts the annual shrimpi festival, celebrating its shrimping industry with food, parades, and live entertainment. The Lydia Anne Lighthouse stands as one of the oldest lighouses in Aransa's Pass. Built in 1857, it guided ships through the challenging waters of the region for nearly a century before being decommissioned. Located in central Texas, the city of Waco gained prominence through its connection to the Chisum Trail, the birthplace of Dr. Peppa and its contributions to education from Baylor University, one of the oldest universities in Texas. Waco also became internationally recognized due to events like the 1993 siege of a compound belonging to the religious doomsday cult known as the Branch Devidians by the US federal government and Texas state law enforcement officials. This was a 51-day standoff that culminated in a fire that destroyed the compound and resulted in 76 casualties, including David Caresh, the group's leader. Completed in 1870, the Waco suspension bridge was the first major suspension bridge in Texas, spanning the Brazos River. The bridge was built using cables supplied by John Robing and Company, the same firm that later constructed the Brooklyn Bridge. After serving vehicles for over a century, it was converted to pedestrian use in 1971 and now serves as a centerpiece for community events. Big Ben National Park spans a massive area which includes desert, mountains, and river environments. The name Big Bend comes from the sharp curve in the Rio Grand where the river takes a dramatic bend as it flows through the area. The park was established in the mid 1900s, but the area has a long history of human activity with remnants of early Native American civilizations such as the Jonard Molon and later Apache groups. It is home to landmarks such as the Rio Grand and the northern portion of the Chihuahuan Desert. The Rio Grand is a defining feature of Big Ben National Park, carving deep canyons and serving as an international boundary as it provides water for wildlife and supports ecosystems along its banks. It is also a natural border between Texas and Mexico. Visitors often explore the Rio Grand through float trips using kayaks or rafts. The Chihuahuan Desert covers much of Big Bend National Park and is one of North America's largest deserts. Despite the dry, harsh conditions, the desert is home to a wide variety of life and a range of animal species. In fact, the flora and fauna of the Chihuahuan Desert are particularly adapted to extreme conditions with many plants and animals having evolved special traits for water conservation and temperature regulation. The desert landscape is also marked by distinct landforms such as meases and dry riverbeds that create vivid contrasts between the sparse foliage and the rugged terrain. Balanced rock is a unique geological formation located in the grapevine hills area of Big Bend National Park. It features a massive boulder delicately balanced at top two smaller rocks. The coastal town of Leaport played an important role in early settlement efforts and later became a hub for industry and recreation. Its location near Trinity Bay and the Houston Ship Channel has shaped its development over the years. Built in the early 1900s, the SS Texas is a historic battleship that played a significant role in both military and commercial activities. Used in both World Wars, the ship was involved in both military campaigns and commercial shipping during its service. The ship was eventually decommissioned and preserved as a historical piece serving as a museum and a tribute to the naval history of the region. The Sanjasinto monument commemorates the battle of Sanjasinto, the decisive conflict that secured independence during the Texas Revolution. Built between 1936 and 1939, the monument features an art deco design topped with a 220 ton star. At 567 ft tall, it is the tallest masonry column in the world. The Sanjasinto River flows into Trinity Bay near Laort and has been vital for trade and transportation. It is located near the monument dedicated to the battle of Located at its mouth, Morgan's Point has been around as an early settlement site. Named after James Morgan, who established a colony there in 1834, Morgan's Point later became known as the Gold Coast due to its popularity among wealthy residents. According to legend, folk heroine Emily West from the song Yellow Rose of Texas played a role in delaying Mexican General Santa Anna at Morgan's Point before his defeat at Sanjasinto. The area was also a key location for ferry crossings until modern bridges were built. The Fred Hartman Bridge is a cablestate bridge that spans the Houston Ship Channel connecting Leaport to Baytown. Opened in 1995, it features twin diamond-shaped pylons that support eight lanes of traffic between Baytown and Leaport. The bridge replaced the Baytown tunnel, which had become inadequate for larger ships using the channel. Due to the state being prone to natural disasters, the bridge was built with a design that efficiently withstands hurricane force winds while maintaining aesthetic appeal. The Blanco River is a scenic waterway consisting of clear springfed waters and serves as a defining feature of the Texas Hill Country. It flows through limestone cliffs and rolling terrain, feeding into aquifers like the Edwards Aquifer and therefore supplying water to springs such as Barton Springs and San Marcos Springs. These aquifers are critical for both human consumption and endangered species habitats. Spanish explorers named the river Blanco, meaning white, in 1721 due to the bright limestone cliffs and stream bed that line its banks. The state capital Austin was originally called Waterloo when it was selected as the site for the capital of the Republic of Texas in 1839. The name was later changed to honor Steven F. Austin, known as the father of Texas for his role in leading American settlers to the region. Nicknamed the live music capital of the world, it is home to a thriving music scene. Outside of being a political center, it hosts iconic festivals like South by Southwest, SXSW, and Austin City Limits and a creative atmosphere that attracts artists, entrepreneurs, and innovators. The city is also recognized for its tech industry growth, its motto, keep Austin weird, and its enthusiastic food culture. The Texas State Capital serves as the seat of government for the state and is the largest state capital building in the US. Completed in 1888, the capital was built using pink granite from nearby Marble Falls. At the time of its completion, it was taller than the US capital in Washington DC. The building was funded through a land swap involving 3 million acres in the Texas panhandle which became the XIT ranch. Visitors can explore its chambers, including the Senate and House of Representatives, as well as the Rotunda, which houses one of the largest domes in the US. The capital also serves as a gathering place for protests, public events, and celebrations. Lake Austin is a reservoir on the Colorado River that provides opportunities for boating and fishing. It is part of the Highland Lake system managed by the Lower Colorado River Authority. In fact, it was created in 1939 with the construction of Tom Miller Dam, replacing an earlier reservoir destroyed by flooding. The area around the lake has developed into a desirable neighborhood with upscale homes offering views of the water. The Penny Backa Bridge, also known as the 360 bridge, spans Lake Austin on Loop 360 Highway. It serves as a vital connection between the northern and southern parts of the city. Completed in 1982, it features a weathering steel arch designed to blend with its natural surroundings and is representative of Austin's unique architecture today. The bridge was built without scaffolding over the lake to protect its environment. Its location offers an elevated vantage point, allowing those who drive across or walk along it to appreciate the surrounding natural beauty. Galveastston Island is a barrier island located on the Gulf Coast known for its resilience in the face of natural disasters such as the devastating hurricane of 1900. By the mid-9th century, Galveastston became one of Texas's largest cities and a leading port for cotton exports. It was also an entry point for immigrants arriving from Europe and continued to serve as a major immigration port for decades, second only to Ellis Island in New York. The historic Hotel Galves is an iconic beachfront hotel that has been welcoming guests since 1911. Known as the Queen of the Gulf, it is celebrated for its architecture and connection to Galveastston's recovery after the 1900 hurricane. The East End Lagoon Preserve is one of the last large tracts of undeveloped coastal prairie on Galveastston Island, though it is recognized for its ecological importance. The preserve encompasses a variety of ecosystems and is an important stopover for migratory birds along the Gulf Coast. Managed by local organizations, plans are underway to expand trails and enhance access while protecting its fragile environment. Scattered across the state, the painted churches of Texas are renowned for their intricately decorated interiors, showcasing the craftsmanship and artistry of 19th century immigrants from Germany, the Czech Republic, and other Slavic regions. These churches feature dynamic murals, stenciling, faux marble columns, and stained glass, transforming simple wooden structures into elaborate spaces reminiscent of Gothic cathedrals in Europe. They remain active places of worship and are celebrated for preserving the cultural identity of immigrant communities. Flowing through the Texas Hill Country, Pedanalis River is a central feature of Pedanalis Falls State Park. Its name comes from the Spanish word for flint, reflecting the flintstones found along the river banks, which were historically important to local Native American tribes for tool making. It is also recognized for its rapid changes in water levels making it both a tranquil and powerful force of nature. This is Texas, a land of hidden wonders and remarkable architectural marvels. Now, if you don't want to miss the most unreal wonders of Alaska, check out this video.

- Texas's power disaster is a warning sign for the US: Kind: captions Language: en-US In mid-February, cold arctic air, usually contained up north in the Earth’s jet stream, dipped down into the US. It’s called a polar vortex, and it’s pretty common. It’s what brings the coldest air to the US during the winter. What was unusual, is for it to dip as far south as it did. In Texas, where February is typically fairly warm, temperatures dipped dangerously low. And it came with a storm. More than 4 million Texans lost power. Many wouldn’t get it back for nearly five days. By then, pipes had frozen. People were melting snow for drinking water. And dozens had died from the cold in their cars and in their homes. And it was almost even worse. The state’s entire electrical grid was within “seconds and minutes” of a failure that would’ve lasted months. But even though this storm hit this huge area, it was really just Texas that had major loss of power. There’s a reason for that. And it should be a warning sign for the rest of the US. When you flip your lights on, you’re getting your electricity from the power grid. And that grid is getting the electricity from a bunch of different energy systems. Like nuclear power plants, or wind farms, or natural gas facilities. In the US, these places are generally run by private energy companies, that work with your local power utility, which directs the electricity around the grid and into your home. That power utility is overseen by a government commission, who sets things like prices and safety standards. And the grids most Americans are on aren’t just big. They’re huge. The entire east coast, from Canada to Florida, is on one grid. And the entire west coast is connected in another. It's kind of a remarkable engineering feat. It's probably the biggest machine humans have ever built. While most power tends to stay local, this grid allows electricity to flow over huge areas, and between states. Like, in New York City, power comes from energy plants in New York, but also from neighboring states, and even Canada. This huge grid also means that, in emergencies, power can be directed where it’s needed. Like in Oklahoma, which was also hit bad during the February winter storm. They needed more power. And instead of completely losing it, they were able to get electricity from neighboring states through the grid. But it’s not so seamless to send it to Texas. Texas opted out of the big grids, back when they were being built, nearly a hundred years ago. Since they covered several states, it meant the federal government could regulate them. Texas was not into that. Texas decided to kind of go it alone. It's a very uniquely Texas-type thing to do. So most of Texas is on its own grid. It means they don’t have to follow the same regulations as the rest of the country, like reliability and cost standards. But it also means when it lost power in the storm, it couldn’t easily get electricity from other states. Its independence kept it in the dark. But it wasn’t the reason it went dark in the first place. Being on its own, Texas has to make all their own power. They have a variety of sources. When the storm came, it affected every one. Several coal plants stopped operating, as coal piles froze. One of four Texan nuclear plants went offline. Some wind turbines stopped working in the freezing cold. But the biggest failure, in terms of its magnitude and its impact, was the natural gas system. Natural gas, which actually contains water vapor, froze in storage wells and pipelines. Every one of these systems failed, in part. And it had nothing to do with the type of energy, and everything to do with these companies not being prepared for the cold. Clearly, you can run an energy system in cold temperatures. There are wind turbines operating in Antarctica, and gas plants in Alaska and Alberta. So this is not a technology-specific issue. It was more a failure to anticipate that this is something that could even occur in Texas at this length and severity, and to prepare for extreme cold temperatures. But it should have been anticipated. Because this has happened in Texas before. In 1989, the same kind of arctic storm caused natural gas plants to have the same problems, resulting in rolling blackouts. And the same thing again, in 2011. And both times, federal commissions recommended that utilities "ensure reliability in extreme weather conditions." And specifically, for the state government to create winterization standards for these energy sources. The Texas utility commission did make those standards -- but also made them voluntary. So most companies didn’t winterize. Private companies like these don’t have an incentive to spend their money preparing for unpredictable and infrequent events without being required to. But this problem is not limited to Texas. The particular vulnerability to the extreme cold may be a uniquely Texas thing. But I think what it shows, is you need to check your blind spots. And that applies everywhere. This is a chart of the last 40 years of severe weather disasters in the US. Storms in particular are in orange. Here's the Texas winter storm in 1989, and the one in 2011. These costly disasters are becoming more frequent -- not just in Texas, but nationally, and globally. And the US is not prepared. Most of this grid was built 60-70 years ago, but was only built to last 50 years. And some studies point to nearly 100 facilities, like power plants, in immediate danger of flooding and storm surges. Most of these private energy companies, across the US, aren’t going to change unless they’re required to. But these utility commissions can require it. As can state governments. And the federal government. All three can even help pay for it. Texas was warned for 30 years to prepare its energy systems for severe weather and climate change. But they didn’t. Now the rest of the US is facing the same problem. It is a wakeup call, for everywhere, to think about, how do we prepare our energy infrastructures, and our other critical infrastructure, for extreme events that may become more severe, and may become more likely? And that isn't about just thinking about what's probable, but also, what can break the system in a way that's catastrophic?

Luckily before winter, we disconnected and put away all the outdoor garden hoses, we wrapped up tight all 3 of our hose spigots with foam pipe insulation, weatherproof tape to tie and lock it in tight against the faucet, and a faucet sock over the wrapped up faucet to protect it from the snow and add additional protection against freezing, including our main shutoff valve and main water lines. We also were fully prepared and we had a long lasting supply of non perishable food, two 40-pack cases of bottled water for me, my mom, and our cat, cat food, LED flashlights and extra batteries, our devices were fully charged before the power turned off, including our portable battery powered chargers for keeping our phones charged so we can be entertained and most importantly stay updated on the weather, power outage statuses, severe weather alerts, and we had TONS of big blankets, warm clothes and jackets, and we even stole the comforters from the bed in the guest bedroom and used them for my mom to wrap herself up and use her blanket and I gave her 3 more big soft plush blankets for her to stay warm especially at night when we were sleeping in the 50 degree house. We also filled up our bathtubs in our bathrooms so we can have water for sanitary purposes, such as flushing toilets, including the supply of toilet paper as well. We were bundled up, warm, safe, and fed during the storm. We were among the luckiest in Texas, as millions of other people were not so prepared like us, especially when their family members were dying of hunger, dehydration, and hypothermia in their homes, not to mention also frozen and busted pipes flooding their homes with freezing water. We survived the storm completely safe and our pipes were not frozen, house was dry and not as cold inside, due to the good insulation. This was a pretty rough storm for all of us particularly in Texas.

- Climate Lessons of the Texas Freeze: Kind: captions Language: en what made this particular event so severe was that normal arctic outbreak that we expect to get pretty much every winter was made colder stronger longer deeper south because it was reinforced by a breakdown of the stratospheric polar vortex the vortex on a typical winter doesn't really have any effect on our weather down here in the united states but every so often about every two years on average something will disrupt the polar vortex wind exists because of temperature differences warm air expands so if you were sitting on top of a layer of air that extended from say new york up to the arctic and looking towards the north pole it would look like that layer of air was tilting downhill and so just like water wanting to flow down the side of a mountain that air starts to flow down that hill towards the arctic so that creates a south to north wind but because the earth is spinning it turns that wind to the right and so we end up with a west to east wind going around the northern hemisphere and that is what we call the jet stream when the jet stream becomes very wavy for whatever reason and by wavy i mean taking these big northward swings and southward dips that wave energy can travel upward and start poking the stratospheric polar vortex you can think of the polar vortex as a spinning top and if you start poking at a spinning top eventually that top is going to fall off its spindle in this particular case it made it split and when that happens we know that we're going to get some crazy weather these breakdowns of the stratospheric vortex happen naturally but there is more and more scientific evidence that these things are starting to happen more often and we believe that there are some climate change connections the jet stream of course is a very complicated thing many factors can cause it to be wavier the fact that the arctic is warming so much faster than anywhere else about three times Joshua Tree was horrible. Not at all how it happened. I got an Airbnb that was completely made of glass where I was like surrounded by this lonely desert life that I was living and I was struggling a lot with like what ended up being an official OCD diagnosis but some like really bad symptoms OCD and just a lot of like very not in reality thoughts and feelings that were really scary and hard to deal with. And I realized that like I was, not only was I not going to be healed by going somewhere traditionally beautiful or you know foreign, but I also wasn't, I really wasn't in a good place mentally outside of just about making music. I was really struggling to get through every day. That trip made me realize that I couldn't just run from shit and I had to actually deal with a little bit of it. It was like the trip that that inspired me to finally get back on medication and go start looking at these OCD symptoms and also to step away from music. I called my manager and I was just like, dude, I'm here. I fucking hate it. The trees sucks, trees are great. Like the trail's amazing. A lot of people would cut off t-shirts and stuff. It's amazing but I, it's not doing anything for me and I don't think I'm the kind of person that like needs to go somewhere new. I think I need to like figure out what the fuck I I'm doing mentally. - Yeah. And with your OCD diagnosis, was this like through therapy online where you literally in the desert when you found out like how did this work? - I go to therapy via Zoom every week. - Okay. - And so I had just been having like a pretty severe episode where I was convinced something was happening that just was not happening or that I had said something that I just didn't say or that I'm like I could convince myself that I had ran somebody over knowing for a fact that I didn't see another person on the road. And I was in that moment where I'm just like, oh my God, what did I do? I'm reassuring seeking from other people around me. I'm like, did I say that? Did I do that? And it's like I've seen it scare people around me where they're like, no, no that didn't happen. But like in my head I'm like, nothing they say is gonna make me feel better. The music became this weird obsession where like every time I started something I would just convince myself that it was horrible or that I'd done it before and not really in a, a way that I have before where it's just like I'm having creative issues. It was like this really insidious feeling of like this is bad, this is bad, this is bad. Stop, stop, stop. This voice in my head was just making me feel like I was writing nonsense on the page. And then I was dealing with these real life things where I couldn't remember conversations and days of my life because I had been so caught in this tangle of thoughts. And so my therapist had been kind of like, I just registering this for a long time but I think really like, let me know what I was going through was was OCD and it wasn't like just anxiety or being stressed. It was like this real insidious like disease or sickness or disorder that I was struggling with. - East Texas medical professionals remind people about threat of hypothermia, frostbite: Kind: captions Language: en Thank you for being here with me today. Uh we got this winter weather approaching. What do people need to know to stay safe? >> Sure. So, you know, hypothermia is something that can occur very quickly and unexpectedly. Uh temps don't have to be sub-zero to get hypothermic. uh patients can get hypothermic in temperatures of even 40° or uh the elderly and our pediatric population are at risk for hypothermia just being in a cold room or a cold home. Uh some of the things that are really concerning about hypothermia is sometimes the patient doesn't know that they're becoming hypothermic. uh it tends to cause confusion and patients we call it the umbles. They get stumbling, fumbling, mumbling and grumbling because they're not able to think properly and they can't function properly. So sometimes we'll have hypothermic patients. They might refuse help because they don't know that they are becoming hypothermic and they are in grave danger. So, one of the things that we have to do is we have to quickly identify that there's hypothermia going on and then we want to rapidly warm them. But you want to be gradual in how you warm them up so you don't cause any sort of rapid changes in the patient's body temperature that can lead to lead to complications. >> Yeah. Why? Two things going off of that, but I'll start out. you know why is it that gradual warming and not the um and not like instant I guess. >> Sure. Because whenever you you warm too quickly you get shifts sometimes in warm fluid that get shifted to the periphery of the body. And sometimes it can cause a a decrease in your core body temperature as all the warm fluid gets uh gets shifted to the periphery. And so you have to be somewhat careful as you're warming patients back up. >> So if you don't gradually warm someone, it can actually make them colder. >> It potentially can. It potentially can. The other thing you want to be really careful about with hypothermic patients is the cardiac membranes are quite irritable. So you want to prevent sudden jolts or rough handling of these patients because it can trigger arrhythmias uh with their heart and put them into a fatal arrhythmia. >> What are some of the symptoms of hypothermia? I know you kind of alluded to the fact it can be tough to deduce, but are there any symptoms you >> Sure. Sure. So the first line of defense that your body has is shivering. Uh, that's how you know you're being exposed to extreme cold and your body is doing what it can to try to warm itself up. At about 90 degrees, at at 95 degrees Fahrenheit, your body starts uh >> body temperature. >> Yes. A body temperature of 95 degrees is where your body starts losing heat faster than it can produce. And so it'll start shivering and it'll start shifting blood from the periphery to the core to try to keep itself warm. Once your body temperature drops below 90 degrees, uh most patients are no longer able to shiver and that's a very dangerous point as your body is no longer able to keep itself warm and from there you develop confusion as you start getting a decrease in mental functioning as well as physical functioning which leads to confusion and at that point patients may not be aware of what's going on to them. And what's the difference between hypothermia and frostbite? >> Sure. So hypothermia is a a general decrease in a patient's body temperature and it affects all parts of their body. In frostbite you get specific cold injuries to particular parts of the body and it's usually it's going to be on your tips. So on your nose tip, your chin, your fingertips, your toes. And the reason they get frostbite is because as the blood is shifting towards the the core of the body and away from the periphery, you lose blood flow to these extremities and they start freezing. And so you will develop ice crystals literally in the cells and in the blood vessels that causes damage. So there's three stages of frostbite. There's [clears throat] frost knit which is just you know a superficial uh injury. There's superficial frostbite, which is stage two, and then you have deep frostbite, which is stage three, in which there's there's more extensive damage. >> And is it pretty visible when it's >> Yes. Yes. It's going to look pale. Uh there may not be good color there. It may appear dusky at times. And in severe frostbite, you also develop very large blisters. >> So when people are, you know, as this winter weather's approaching, what When should people know, you know, I have this or I see this, I should probably go see a medical professional. >> Absolutely. You know, at any concerns because this can be really difficult to to know at times, but if you if you notice a family member or friend, they've been in a cold environment for a prolonged period of time and you notice changes to their skin color, you notice changes in their behavior, you should immediately call 911 and seek help. And what are and that applies to both hypothermia and frostbite. >> That's correct. And some of the things that they can do uh if they suspect hypothermia um if the patient is in any sort of wet clothes or has been exposed to any sort of moisture, get them out of the wet clothes, get them dry, and wrap them in blankets and warm clothing to to try to passively warm them gradually. Um, and when it comes to frostbite, we tend to try to rub our tips of fingertips or whatever to try to rewarm them. And you want to avoid that in any sort of frostbite injury as it can cause further damage. So, you want to just uh apply passive uh warming techniques uh with clothing and get them to a hospital so they can be reworn properly.

- 5 Cool Tips For Helping You Manage Grazing Your Livestock This Winter On Your Farm 🥶: Kind: captions Language: en-GB Winter can be a challenging time for managing slow pasture growth rates, wet soils, short day lengths, cold weather. In this video, we’ll discuss five tips to help you manage your winter grazing. Hi, I’m Oliver from Pasture dot i o. Let’s get into these five tips. The first tip is to get on top of your grazing management. The two most significant areas to focus on are pasture leaf emergence and growth rate. So, determine the days required to bring your pasture to full leaf emergence and plan your grazing schedule accordingly. Your pastures will be vegetative throughout the winter, and this is the time to grow pastures to full leaf emergence without sacrificing quality. Not only will your animals be happy, but maintaining your post-grazing residuals will set you up for a bumper spring flush. Match your livestock's pasture growth rate demand with the growth rate of your pastures. This will ensure optimal pre-grazing cover and post-grazing residuals, resulting in maximum plant recovery and growth rates. If you’re wondering, these tools are in Pasture.io and are free to use to help you get on top of your area allocation and grazing rotations. Okay, the second tip revolves around the quality of forage. Because of the colder temperatures, forage quality can deteriorate. On top of additional animal requirements, adverse weather can increase forage wastage. This means that grazing animals must consume more forage to meet their nutritional requirements, which compounds the need for high-quality forage. Here are some pointers to help you make sure your animals are getting enough forage. Ensure your pasture is managed correctly by following the suggestions I mentioned. Overgrazing can harm grasses, making them less nutritious and more difficult for animals to digest, and this is exacerbated by plants struggling to produce enough feed, leading to further overgrazing. Select forage plants with high quality and digestibility. Lucerne, alfalfa, clover, and grasses like ryegrass and tall fescue are all excellent choices. Moving onto the third tip of supplementary feeding. Feed your animals fodder to relieve stress on your forage system. Hay, silage, and other supplemental feed are excellent sources for supplementing the feeding system. When your forages or pastures are too wet, prepare a standoff area where cattle can safely loaf and consume fodder and other supplements. Choose an appropriate grazing area. Avoid heavily trafficked areas and areas with a lot of standing water, as these can cause health problems for your cows while significantly increasing the wastage of expensive supplements. It is critical to supplement your animal's diet with hay or other forage or supplements when grazing in the winter. This will assist your livestock in maintaining a healthy body condition while enabling you to match demand with the pasture growth rate. And remember, these tools are in Pasture.io and are free to use. We’ve focused a lot on feeding animals. Now let’s look at the fourth tip, which concerns the drinking water supply. A readily available, clean drinking water supply is essential for all livestock. Make a plan to keep your animals hydrated, whether using permanent stock troughs, transporting water with portable troughs, or getting creative with temporary fencing. If you use temporary fencing, ensure that the temporary front and back fencing can easily work around the drinking points. This is critical for enhancing pasture utilisation. In other words, providing water points in strategic locations will reduce the amount of walking livestock must do over pastures and laneways. This is part of your fencing and area allocation plans and brings us to the fifth tip, strategic fencing. Fencing or temporary fencing made of standards or pigtail posts and nylon wire is essential in allocating the proper area for your livestock. Make sure that the grazing area is securely fenced. Cows can be very destructive when grazing, so you'll need to keep them under control. You'll want to ensure your livestock don't break out and ruin the subsequent feed allocations. This will only delay and harm your grazing strategy. Check that your fencing is in good shape and can withstand winter weather. And make sure your fences are carrying enough electrical current to deter livestock from pushing the fence over. In saying that, your livestock shouldn’t be that hungry. Plan your grazing carefully so that your pasture is rotated correctly and no areas are overgrazed. Back fence with temporary fencing to allow your grazed pastures time to recover. Keep an eye on the weather forecast and graze accordingly, giving your pasture a break if it rains heavily. Pugging and muddying pastures will result in damaged soils, wasted pastures, and reduced pasture growth rates. So that is how you can use temporary fencing to avoid and mitigate the pitfalls of grazing during the cold and wet winter. Until next time, happy grazing!

  Top Comments:
  1. @roger80465 [6 likes]: We've wintered in Mission, TX for 8 years.  Yes, it's breezy here.  I joke that anything less than 25 mph is considered a breeze.  But that breeze also reduces flying insects so you don't have to worry about mosquitos or sand fleas unless the wind quits.  You can sit outside and enjoy friends without getting eaten alive.  Yes, when it rains here, it seriously rains but most of the winter it is dry.  The locals really appreciate Winter Texans because their 2 major industries in the Rio Grande Valley are citrus and Winter Texans.  We have been approached by locals in a restaurant just to thank us for coming to the RGV.  Educators love Winter Texans because they show their students that if they work hard, get an education and plan ahead, they can live like this when they get older and not have to work well into their 80s. There are around 300 RV parks in the area so you can camp in a park that shares your interests. Our first couple years in this park, we paid $375 a month plus electricity for a double wide lot with an unfinished casita that contained laundry, a refrigerator, additional bathroom, and patio furniture.  Where we winter, there are 8 parks within a short golf cart ride, and we can participate in any of their activities as well as those in our park.  If you get bored, it's your own fault.  We love it here and wouldn't consider Florida or Arizona.
