- The 7 Stages Of Financial Freedom: Kind: captions Language: en-US What do you think about when you hear the words Financial Independence? What does it mean to you? In truth, financial freedom can mean different things to different people. Most people believe that it means having enough money to avoid working unless you want to, and that there are many paths to get there. When the time comes to retire, we must have a plan to continue living comfortably. Do you understand where you’ll need to be financially to ensure you can retire comfortably? Do you think that you will get to old age without having to work? Do you even know where you are now and where you need to get to? If not, it may be a good starting point for you to start planning for your own financial independence. Hello and welcome back to the channel! Financial Freedom can be a daunting concept as a whole, but today we’ll break it down into its seven stages. These are meant to simply inform and give you an idea of where you may be right now, and the possible future stages you’d want to ensure you hit as you prepare for retirement or changes in your financial journey. So, let's jump right in. Stage 1: Dependency This is the exact opposite of financial independence, but we all start here. This stage marks where your expenses are higher than your income. You may need others to help you keep afloat, or you may rely on loans to get you from paycheck to paycheck. Whatever the dependency, this stage requires you to evaluate your situation closely. You need to get a clear picture of where you are and make a clear plan for where you want to be. You must check your credit scores and log in to your bank and credit card accounts. You'll also have to do the unpleasant work of getting your bills together and adding up how much you spend each month. Make a financial plan and take charge of your money; it’s essential in these changing times. Remember to keep a check on your spending and investments. This is where you should begin to keep track of your net worth, spending, and assets. This will give you a better idea of where you stand and potential paths out of clearing your debts. Use this stage to get into the habit of setting spending and saving targets. Work to increase your ability to earn more income, either by taking on side hustles or increasing your skill set. The dependency stage can often feel defeating, especially as an adult, but you can move past it with intention and determination. Stage 2: Self-Sufficiency This is the point where you can begin to consider yourself independent. As of right now, you are most likely not getting assistance from parents or the government. You’re able to meet and pay your monthly bills. Now, you’re probably still living paycheck to paycheck, and in debt, but you're no longer dependent. Even though it may not feel that way. The most important concept is that you’re now in charge of your financial life. Many financial experts also call this stage solvency. You’re making enough to keep yourself afloat. Saving and investing might not yet be an option, but you can take care of yourself and your needs month to month. Stage 3: Stability At this level, you can finally start saving money. You succeed in breaking the cycle of living from paycheck to paycheck and all the stress that comes with it. Many would say this is the stage at which you first begin to save your emergency fund. Save $1,000 right away. Consider this the first step of your emergency fund regardless of other bills or obligations you may have. Make this a priority! Licensed financial advisor Adrienne Taylor-Wells from Tailored WealthSaver stresses having cushion money in light of the current high rate of inflation, which is the highest it has been in 41 years. While you're working to pay off your debt, your emergency fund will keep you from reaching for your credit card in an emergency. Access to cash is essential in emergency situations like a breakdown of your vehicle, roof leak, or medical issue. Remember, this initial fund should only be used for unexpected expenses, not for entertainment or discretionary spending. Stage 4: Security If you're still living paycheck to paycheck, saving six months' worth of expenses may seem like an impossible task. However, you need to be consistent and patient. The road to financial independence requires intentionality, and getting to level 4, the security stage, generally entails removal of debts and saving of 3-6 months’ worth of expenses. Financial security comes once you’ve eliminated your debt (or at least have the assets to pay off your debt if needed), and you can withstand loss of employment for a short period of time. If you had been consistently saving and investing in stages 2 and 3, then entrance into this stage is assured. This stage you’ve made your money a safety net, but now you can also make it a tool to build your wealth. You may begin to consider investing in other assets such as brokerage accounts, real estate, small businesses, etc. Stage 5: Flexibility After you've built up a solid rainy-day fund and removed most, if not all debt from your life, you can now start heavily investing that extra cash. It's the same principle at work: little by little, day by day, month by month, year by year, the results will add up. This will bring you to stage 5, flexibility. Between stages 4 and 5, you’ll continue to make as much money as you can, save, and invest. Now, your investments get to a point where they can cover most, if not all, of your base essential living expenses. You may not have enough to quit working entirely, but you start to see the fruits of your labor. Advisers like to mark this stage with enough money saved to cover your living costs for 1 to 2 years. Now, you have the freedom to take a year off work to do whatever you want with it. Go on a grand adventure, spend more time with your loved ones, or finish writing that novel you've always wanted to. It grants you true flexibility to remove yourself from work, at least temporarily. Stage 6: Financial Independence At this point, you can finally walk away from your job for good and depend entirely on the profits your investments bring in. Congratulations, this an outstanding achievement and a personal finance success! You can consider yourself financially independent if you make it to stage 6. You could technically retire at any time and not have to worry about how to pay your bills. The amount needed for financial independence varies from person to person because lifestyles, hobbies, and passions differ for everyone. This stage signals that whatever your weekly, monthly, or annual needs, you have enough money saved and invested to cover you for the rest of your life. Stage 7: Financial Abundance This last stage does not need much explanation. You can now jet-set around the world and dine at fine restaurants without looking at the costs because you have more money than you’ll ever need. Congratulations, you've just entered the top one percent. Have fun, and remember to help those in need! -- Financial independence will take time to achieve, but the peace of mind, confidence, and control over your fate will be well worth it. The process is simple, but we’d never say it was easy. If it were, everyone would be financially independent. It will take intentionality and determination, but it's possible! Many people make decent money but never advance past the first several phases. Remember that the decisions you make will help you get there. Financial independence is more of a process than a destination. To make effective decisions, you must know where you are along this path. Keep in mind that the destination is very personal, which is why you must set your own goals that describe the perfect future you are striving for. We hope you enjoyed this information on the stages of financial freedom. Tell us below where you are in your journey! Share this video with your friends and don’t forget to like and subscribe so that you don’t miss out on future videos!

- A Day in the Life of a Family in the Coldest Village on Earth (−64°C, −84°F) Yakutia, Siberia: Kind: captions Language: en 7 AM. In a frozen, remote village&nbsp; in Yakutia, the temperature dropped&nbsp;to -54 degrees Celsius. Despite the&nbsp; chilly weather, life here doesn’t stop. Alyona, a mother of three, begins her&nbsp; morning routine by waking her children. 8-year-old Saiaana, the eldest,&nbsp; is a primary school student,&nbsp;&nbsp; but due to today's extreme cold, the&nbsp; school has closed its doors. Meanwhile,&nbsp;&nbsp; the nursery that 6-year-old Kiunnei&nbsp; attends keeps its schedule unchanged,&nbsp;&nbsp; unaffected by the harsh conditions outside.&nbsp; This means Kiunnei must still go to nursery. Alyona, a middle school teacher, faces the&nbsp; daily challenge of getting her children ready&nbsp;&nbsp; and herself to work on time. Her workday&nbsp; starts at 8 AM, and both the nursery and&nbsp;&nbsp; her school still operate despite the freezing&nbsp; temperatures, meaning Alyona and her children&nbsp;&nbsp; do not have the option to stay at home today. The preparation for leaving the house is meticulous Saiaana, although her school is&nbsp;closed today, dresses warmly to accompany her&nbsp;&nbsp; mother and her siblings to the nursery. The family&nbsp;layers up with thermal wear, multiple sweaters,&nbsp;&nbsp; heavy coats, and accessories that&nbsp; cover every inch of exposed skin.&nbsp; The youngest, 2-year-old Ersan, also&nbsp; attends a local nursery school. In Yakutia,&nbsp;&nbsp; it's typical for children to start&nbsp; daycare as early as 2 months old and&nbsp;&nbsp; continue until they begin primary school at age 7.&nbsp; The family sets out on their&nbsp; cold, dark walk to the nursery. The morning remains dark because the sun&nbsp; only appears for a few hours each day&nbsp;during the winter The children hardly ever see&nbsp; daylight, spending most of their time inside,&nbsp;&nbsp; safe from the severe cold outside. In Yakutia, even a 10-minute walk can be challenging, as the cold&nbsp; seeps through any layer of clothing.&nbsp; Finally, Alyona and her&nbsp; children reach the nursery.&nbsp; The severity of the cold becomes evident as she&nbsp; notices that little Ersan is complaining of pain&nbsp;&nbsp; in his fingers from the cold air—a reminder of&nbsp; the tough conditions these children often face.&nbsp; In Yakutia, nurseries usually serve&nbsp; children from 2 month up to age 7.&nbsp;&nbsp; These centers help teach reading, writing, and&nbsp; basic math before the children start school. Nursery usually operate from 8 AM to 6.30 PM.&nbsp; They are often public, funded by the government,&nbsp;&nbsp; and free of charge, making them accessible to&nbsp; everyone. Meals are also provided and are a&nbsp;&nbsp; crucial part of the day. Children eat&nbsp; four times a day at the nursery school,&nbsp;&nbsp; which includes first breakfast, second breakfast,&nbsp;&nbsp; lunch, and afternoon tea, ensuring they receive&nbsp; enough calories to cope with the harsh winters. Many public buildings, including schools and&nbsp; nurseries, in Yakutian villages use central&nbsp;&nbsp; heating systems, unlike the houses. These&nbsp; systems are usually powered by centralized&nbsp;&nbsp; boilers that distribute heat through a network&nbsp;of pipes, with coal often used as fuel Alyona&nbsp;&nbsp;and her family live in the western part&nbsp; of Yakutia, the coldest region on Earth,&nbsp;&nbsp; home to about ten thousand inhabitants.&nbsp; Here, the winters are long and harsh,&nbsp;&nbsp; yet residents continue their daily routines,&nbsp; braving the frigid temperatures to head to work.&nbsp;&nbsp; Primary schools suspend in-person classes&nbsp; when temperatures drop below -45°C/-49°F,&nbsp;&nbsp; so Saiaana is attending her&nbsp; classes from home today. Despite the sevire cold, Alyona, Saiaana's&nbsp; mother, is at work today. The middle school,&nbsp;&nbsp; unlike the primary schools, remains open, and&nbsp; its students are attending classes in person. Alyona is a music teacher and teaches her&nbsp; classes in the Sakha language, native to&nbsp;&nbsp; the residents of Yakutia. The Sakha language&nbsp; is part of the Siberian Turkic branch of the&nbsp;&nbsp; Turkic languages and is spoken by around 450,000&nbsp;native speakers. Locals are typically bilingual,&nbsp;&nbsp; fluent in both Russian and Sakha. In the afternoon, Radomir,&nbsp; the father of the house,&nbsp;&nbsp; returns from work. He works as a&nbsp; cameraman for a local TV channel. While his wife, Alyona,&nbsp; and their children are out,&nbsp;&nbsp; Radomir ensures that the home&nbsp; stays warm by tending to the stove. During the cold winter days, he fires up the&nbsp; stove four times daily to maintain a comfortable&nbsp;temperature. In addition to the heating stove,&nbsp; Radomir and his family use a heating boiler to&nbsp;&nbsp; warm the house. This setup costs them about&nbsp; $300 each month for heating and electricity. &nbsp; In the village, many families engage in cattle&nbsp; breeding and share the yield with their friends&nbsp;&nbsp; and relatives. Throughout the summer, Radomir&nbsp; helps his relatives with hay harvesting,&nbsp;&nbsp; which is crucial for sustaining their&nbsp; cattle through the winter. In return,&nbsp;&nbsp; Radomir receives meat from his relatives, which&nbsp; will sustain his own family throughout the year. Now, Radomir plans to take the&nbsp; frozen meat to a meat cutting&nbsp;&nbsp; station where it can be properly&nbsp; cut and stored for the upcoming&nbsp;winter Meanwhile, after returning home, Alyona takes&nbsp; over caring for her children. She reads them&nbsp;&nbsp; tales in Sakha language, ensuring they stay&nbsp; connected to their culture and mother tongue. Alyona dedicates most of her time&nbsp; to teaching her kids. She nurtures&nbsp;&nbsp; their musical talents by teaching&nbsp; them to sing and play the piano. Alyona and Radomir believe that providing&nbsp; love, warmth, and kindness is essential&nbsp;&nbsp; to giving their children the best upbringing. In Yakutia, families are usually very close-knit,&nbsp;&nbsp; with parents offering unwavering support to their&nbsp; children throughout their lives. This support&nbsp;&nbsp; often continues as the children start their own&nbsp; families, and when the parents grow older and need&nbsp;&nbsp; care, it is common for them to live with their&nbsp; children who then take care of them. It's uncommon&nbsp;&nbsp; for elderly people to go to nursing homes, and&nbsp; there are only few such facilities available.&nbsp; In the harsh climate of Yakutia, where winter&nbsp; temperatures often plummet to extreme lows,&nbsp;&nbsp; managing the water supply is crucial&nbsp; for both survival and comfort. Nikandr,&nbsp;&nbsp; a dedicated waterman, works every day&nbsp; to provide water for the residents. The cold environment presents&nbsp; unique challenges for water systems.&nbsp;&nbsp; Nikandr stick to the traditional water&nbsp; supply system, getting water from a&nbsp;&nbsp; nearby river. This method continues to be&nbsp; prevalent due to the small population and&nbsp;&nbsp; the challenges of installing modern&nbsp; plumbing, as pipes often freeze. To ensure a reliable source of hot water,&nbsp; many homes and buildings in Yakutia,&nbsp;&nbsp; including Alyona's family home, are&nbsp; equipped with water boilers. These&nbsp;&nbsp; boilers are essential not only for&nbsp; domestic activities such as cooking,&nbsp;&nbsp; cleaning, and bathing but also for heating&nbsp; the home. Alyona pays $25 each time for 2&nbsp;&nbsp; cubic meter of water, and monthly, they use 6&nbsp; cubic meters of water, which is $75 a month.&nbsp; With the water now being delivered, Alyona&nbsp; is set to prepare dinner. Using the beef that&nbsp;&nbsp; Radomir had previously prepared, she begins&nbsp; cooking a hearty beef soup. Soup is a staple&nbsp;&nbsp; in this frigid climate, as it provides essential&nbsp; warmth to both body and soul during the cold days. To save water, Alyona and her children wash&nbsp; the dishes in a bowl. From an early age,&nbsp;&nbsp; Saiaana and Kiunnei have been actively&nbsp; helping around the house. They assist&nbsp;&nbsp;with washing dishes, cleaning, and&nbsp; cooking. Even their younger brother&nbsp;&nbsp; Ersan is learning the importance of&nbsp; contributing to household chores. This evening, Alyona is preparing&nbsp; a nourishing meal consisting of a&nbsp;&nbsp; hearty soup and traditional&nbsp; Yakut pancakes for dinner. This is a glimpse into the life of a family&nbsp; living in one of the harshest environments on&nbsp;&nbsp; Earth, yet they find joy and warmth in their&nbsp; familial bonds and the comfort of their home

- Prepping your home for severe winter weather: Kind: captions Language: en-US can expect more action from the Trump administration in Minneapolis. Chelsea Torres Fox 26 news. All right, Chelsea, thank you for that live report. All right. We know up north it is cold. Well that winter blast might be heading our way. Meteorologists say it’s probably going to happen. So it’s headed for Houston this weekend. And it’s going to require some preparation for homeowners. Well, Fox 26 is Tom Zizka is live now with some seasonal reminders of the things that you should start thinking about right now in anticipation of how cold it might get. Caroline Rashi right now, we mean start thinking about it. So this is a good reminder for people who haven’t had to think about this in a year. A great lesson for people who may have moved to Texas have never had to deal with this because our homes weren’t built for the cold. So let’s get dealing with this because we have to deal with this ourselves. The plumbing first. It comes in from the outside exposed pipes. We don’t want that water to freeze. Shouldn’t have to turn the water off with this. But we don’t want these pipes exposed. There are pads designed to insulate those pipes, but if you don’t have one of these, maybe a packing blanket, blanket and some duct tape can do the trick. Another great thing that I love is get some mulch from the big box store there, and stack that up against the pipes. Several bags of that. It does a great job insulating the pipes from the wind and the cold. And next, if you have a sprinkler system, turn that off and bleed the backflow preventer here. I’m not going to go through the details here. You just need a flat head screwdriver here. Go to Google or whatever search engine you need. It’s very, very simple. You just don’t want this thing freezing because that makes a mess. It’s expensive and you don’t want that, and it’s easy to avoid that. Next are the plants outside. We got a lot of plants that hate the extreme cold temperature. You can cover those to take a look. Every plant is different, but once you get down below the freezing level, I recommend if you’re fond of it, protecting it, and if if in the past it has suffered damage from cold weather. Protect it. Better be safe than sorry. Cover it, cover it, cover it with either a frost cloth like this or a blanket. Things you want to avoid are plastic, which you might think would insulate better, but that plastic, if it’s touching the plant and it’s freezing outside or below freezing, that can actually exacerbate any damage that that plant might suffer. You want to anchor those covers, whether it’s through pins like this or rocks or blocks. You want to anchor that so it doesn’t blow so wind doesn’t get under it. But you really want the the blanket to cover the plant and the earth. You also recommend watering the plants before the freeze comes. Why? Well, things like trees, like especially newly planted trees watering, giving it a deep soak before a freeze. That water. Contrary to what you might think, it’s actually insulating the roots. So so watering is going to help protect those new roots that aren’t established yet. And and especially with a thick layer of mulch that will help insulate those roots, protect them from the freeze. And once all the freezing is done, then you can think about maybe trimming anything that got damaged. Established plants will grow back. I’ve seen it happen. It’s great news, but we got to get through this weekend and any other subsequent freezes as well. So start thinking about this tonight and then you’ve got tomorrow. We should have a decent day tomorrow. And then the weather starts getting crappy. It starts getting rainy Thursday, Friday and then the cold start. So this is the sweet spot to start dealing with this. If you need to buy something, the big box stores will probably be busy. Amazon’s an option to to maybe get some of that weatherizing freeze fabric or whatever you may need. Look into that. I just encourage you to start thinking about it now, so that you will not be caught unawares when the freeze comes this weekend. Taking care of you, DIY, watching your money,

- What Houston can expect this winter: 2025-2026 Southeast Texas outlook: Kind: captions Language: en Fox Local. Welcome back to forecasting with friends everybody. I'm Mike Iscovitz here in the Weather Center talking about the winter outlook. Now, you may have heard us mention yesterday didn't get talked about much because of the heavy rain that we had around here on Monday. But yesterday was the first day of what we call meteorological winter. So what's the deal with that? Why is it meteorological winter versus just winter? Well, there's two days to two ways to sort of define winter. One is by the calendar like December 1st and another is by an astronomical event. So most of the time we talk about the first day of winter being the astronomical winter. So that is the shortest day of the year, shortest amount of daylight, typically in the Northern hemisphere. And it also is the day that the sun is as far south as it's going to get. So the first day of winter for us, the first day of summer in the southern hemisphere is that moment that the sun touches 23.5 degrees south latitude. So when that happens, you've got the Arctic Circle, like in northern parts of Alaska. They don't see any sunshine at all. It's just dark all day. And then in the far southern part of the atmosphere, the sun is out all day. So that's the astronomical first day of winter. And that's usually December 20th 1st or December 22nd. And it's a very specific time that we call the winter solstice. The meteorological. Or you could call it calendar. Winter begins on December 1st and it goes through the end of February. Why do we pick those months? Well, because those are the three coldest months of the year, typically. Almost always. So you've got December, January, February. That's meteorological winter. The astronomical winter will go from like December 21st or 22nd to March 21st or 22nd, and it changes from year to year. So I hope that kind of makes sense to you. So let's talk about the outlook for meteorological winter. So we'll look at the outlook across the country but also the outlook right here in Houston obviously, which is what we want you to know. So turning to the graphics here, I have set up here the typical trends that we see as far as temperatures are concerned. Now why do I say typical trends. Because every La Nina is a little different. Sometimes you have a strong La Nina, which by the way, what is La Nina? Well, the El Nino, La Nina is this back and forth oscillation of pressure and wind, and therefore water temperatures in the Pacific Ocean. And all you need to know is that it affects the jet stream flow across the United States, even though it's way down there in the Pacific and the equator during La Nina winters, which is where the water is cold over off the coast of South America, you end up with this, which is where we typically have the jet stream shifted a little farther to the north and warmer than normal conditions, usually across an area from New Mexico and Texas, back across the Gulf Coast, Louisiana, back over to Florida, and then over to the Carolinas. That's usually what we see. Keep in mind this is, on average, okay. It doesn't mean that you're not going to have some Arctic blast that comes down for 3 or 4 days, and it's extremely cold. That could absolutely happen. And, you know, you think back to like the big freeze of 2021, you know, the Valentine's Day or the President's Day. Winter storm. You know, that happened within the context of the rest of the winter. That was relatively mild. But then you have this huge record setting plunge that could happen. But when we look at this, this is just the overall trend is for warmer than average conditions from Texas to Florida and some colder or cooler than normal conditions across the far northern parts of the upper Plains and the northern parts of the Rocky Mountains, and also some wetter than normal conditions across that part of the country as well. So that's a look at temperature trends. When we look specifically at precipitation outlook. This is what we're looking at for precipitation for similar areas here. - Do NOT Pack for Winter Trip in ONLY a Carry-On Bag: Kind: captions Language: en Today's travel tip video is what not to pack in a carry-on for a winter trip. These are essentials you do not really need to pack if you need to save space and only pack in a carry-on suitcase. The number one item to not pack for a winter trip is sweatshirts for three reasons. One, they are bulky. Two, they take up space in carry-on bags and three, they do not keep in your warmth, your body temperature warm. Instead of bulky sweatshirts, pack thin layers. A cashmere sweater will take up less space and cashmere is lightweight for a carry-on only. And hoodies just add extra bulk when you can really just pack a balaclava ninja mask or a scarf. Wear the correct thermal base layers. Even packing a slip tank to wear under a sweater will give you warmth while still being thin layers to pack in a carry-on. Cuddl Duds work great and are affordable. This is their fleece version. I've had this for almost 10 years. These are my base layers and look how thin these pieces are to pack in a carry-on suitcase. I want you to see how the clothes fit in my carry-on suitcase, but normally I recommend that you can pack more if you use compression cubes. The technology used to make extreme weatherproof clothing is based on research that can help us pack less but more efficiently. I wore jeans for my airport outfit. If you have the right base layers, those will help you out so much no matter what you pack. A classic travel hack is to pack your socks inside your boots. Wear hiking shoes like Merrell or Keen's as part of your airport outfit. Cotton absorbs your sweat and therefore it stays wet. For a winter trip, do not pack your thick pajamas because the houses will be so warm and cozy. I always pack a pair of shorts to wear. Our goal when we are packing for a winter trip is to pack the essentials for a winter trip, to be efficient with the essentials that we do pack.   Top Comments:
  1. @austinbrooks965 [9 likes]: IMO, installing a new roof in the winter is just STUPID! You can still SELL roofs in the winter but just install them in the spring. 

The tar strip must get hot in order to create a good bond. Plus the shingles are brittle as hell in the winter and more liable to crack. 

We all know installation is more difficult and riskier in the winter, so why not make the best choice for the customer and WAIT until the spring to do their roof. 

There’s lots of other ways to make money in the winter: insulation, home repairs and remodeling, installing & removing Christmas lights.
  2. @tship8161 [4 likes]: I have never had issues with CertainTeed  in winter... tamko on the other hand has cost us in the past,  I believe that the amount of DIRECT sunlight while installing is a huge aspect to consider along with other variables in the elements mentioned earlier

  11. @derekhaller7435 [1 likes]: The biggest problem I've seen with winter install is expansion and contraction. When installing them in very cold temperatures, they start to expand as they warm up through spring/summer causing buckling and fish mouthing at seams because they push on each other. I've had to cut a 1/4 inch off of a numerous warm shingles in order for them to fit in the same place as shingles that were installed in the winter. I've been roofing in north idaho for 14 years.
  12. @ronpalmer5226 [1 likes]: Did you call Certainteed? I have been wondering about winter installs for a while! We install in New Hampshire and hold off installing during winter when possible. They also do not have temperature cut off for installation.

I’ve done hundreds of winter installs with no problems. 
Proper air pressure, proper nail depth adjustment with the air nailers are all factors.

- Winter in Russia vs Texas 🇷🇺❄️🇺🇸 | How Russians Survive −30°C!: Kind: captions Language: en Hi all. Today's podcast/video is for beginners in Russian. I'll use simple words to convey my story in Russian. Today's topic is winter in Russia versus winter in Texas where I currently live. Ready? Let's go. Hi, my name is Katya. I'm Russian. I'm from Russia. I currently live in Texas. And you know what? Winter here is, so to speak, very interesting. Let me tell you. Winter in Russia and winter in Texas are two different winters. Winter in Russia. Winter in Russia begins in November, sometimes in October. For example, in Khabarovsk, where I am from, it’s already winter. Winter ends in March, sometimes in April, depending on the location. If this is the south of Russia, then winter ends earlier. If north, then later. But overall, yes, 5 months of winter. 5 months. Temperature -10, -20, -30, -40 degrees Celsius. In Khabarovsk, for example, temperatures of -30-40° in winter are normal. It's an ordinary Tuesday. There is snow everywhere. Snow in November, snow in December, snow in January. There is snow in February, sometimes even in March and April. Snow, snow, snow. White, cold snow. You get up in the morning, look out the window, and there’s snow. You go to work, it's snowing. Coming back from work, snow. Every day is the same: snow and cold. And Russian people, we are normal, we are used to it. -30 outside. Come on , no problem. It's all about how to dress properly. We dress like astronauts. First tights, then leggings. This is a type of thermal underwear. Then warm socks, then warm, high boots made of real leather and fur. And on top of your regular clothes, you also need a sweater, then a fur coat, then a warm fur hat, sometimes even two hats, but that’s a must. And what is needed is not a fashionable hat, but a real Russian hat. Another scarf, a long scarf to wrap around your neck. No gloves, it's too cold. Mittens or mittens are better. These thick mittens are knitted or fur. And when you get dressed, you are not a person, you are a tank. But at least it's not cold. And wind, and blizzard, and snow. Now it doesn't matter. You can walk, you can stand at the bus stop and wait for the bus. The most important thing is that you are warm, you are not cold. What about cars? Mm, you can drive a car, right? Mm, you say, cars in winter in Russia are a problem, it’s a quest. You go out in the morning and your car is a snow sculpture. First you clear the snow for 20 minutes, then you warm up the car for 15 minutes. Then you drive slowly, because the roads are like a skating rink, there is ice everywhere. 45 minutes to work. No, now it's 2 hours. This is Russian winter. This is an extreme sport. And now it's winter in Texas. Texas. Sweet, warm, quaint Texas. Winter in Texas is a comedy. The temperature in winter is +15, er, maybe zero. Sometimes. It's not winter, it's spring. For people in Russia, 15° is the month of May. But in Texas, 15° is already cold. People wear jackets. If the temperature is zero, then it's panic. The first time I saw it, I didn't understand. It's sunny outside, the weather is nice, plus 5°, but everyone dresses as if it were the Arctic. Huge jackets, hats, scarves, gloves. I think, guys, it's not cold, it's cool. But for Texas, it's the end of the world. Why? Well, because it's very hot here in other months and there are no cleaning machines on the roads. Driving when it snows is very dangerous. And on the roads this is, of course, no longer funny, it’s dangerous. In Russia, for example, everyone changes their tires to winter tires. In Texas, snow only falls for 2-3 days. And, of course, no one will change tires or clean the roads. This is a big danger and a big problem. That 's why in Texas, winter is the end of the world. People don't drive cars, schools are closed, everyone is buying a ton of groceries at the store. Neighbors say that animals should not be allowed outside. Because they might freeze. I don't agree with this. Animals will not freeze at +5 or even 0 degrees. But in Texas it snows 2-3 days a week maximum, but I won’t argue, so okay, these 2-3 days a year when it snows, but it’s better, really, to sit at home and not argue, because people have chaos, panic , apocalypse. Catastrophe. The funniest thing is that people actually go to buy water, toilet paper, bread. It's as if winter will last for several months. It's just a few days. But you know what? It's better to have winter 3 days a year and panic than winter 5 months a year. So Texas, my choice is yours. Now I think about my grandmother in Siberia and that it is very cold there now, and my grandmother has to walk to the store for bread. So winter is, of course, good, but 3 days a year is quite normal. Friends, that's all from me for today. Thanks for listening and goodbye.

Mount Kenya maybe we'll you will see some snow but that's honestly it's my understanding that that's the farthest that it goes right that's why you'll see the snow right at the Peck and that's it but in other areas snow was not a thing but I remember watching videos uh from the US while in Kenya and seeing um snow and um Christmas they would have snow and kids playing skiing on the snow and I
